content:
14 min read
Just now

--

The interest bill doesn’t wait for the transformer — a view from the country that took a decade to admit its losses

In Japan, we don’t say “the Global Financial Crisis.” We say Lehman Shock — リーマン・ショック. The name of one American bank became the name of an era.
So when a program on one of Japan’s major TV networks — produced in partnership with Bloomberg — asked this week whether AI datacenter debt marks the eve of another Lehman, it wasn’t lazy headline-writing. It’s how this country processes credit anxiety: through the memory of a specific September morning in 2008, and behind it, a longer memory. Our own.
The Bank for International Settlements recently sized up America’s AI investment boom and reached for a yardstick. Considerably smaller, it noted, than Japan’s commercial real estate investment of the 1980s. I read that sentence differently than most BIS readers do. I live in the country being used as the unit of measurement.
Here is what the yardstick doesn’t tell you. Japan’s crisis never had a Lehman morning. No single collapse, no single date. The losses were real, but recognizing them took more than a decade, because we kept finding ways not to look. Banks rolled over loans to borrowers who couldn’t pay, then lent them more to cover the interest. Japanese has a dedicated word for this: oigashi — literally, “chasing loans.” English needs a whole phrase: extend and pretend.
I thought about that word while reading the Financial Stability Board’s latest report on private credit. Payment-in-kind interest, quietly converting cash coupons into larger principal. “Selective defaults” that don’t count as defaults. Quarterly marks on loans that never trade. None of this is fraud. None of it was fraud in Japan, either. It is simply what credit looks like when everyone has an incentive to let the clock run.
And the clocks in this story run at very different speeds. A new transmission line takes four to eight years. Forty percent of US datacenters planned to open this year are running at least three months late. Private credit funds mark their books once a quarter. Interest switched to PIK becomes next quarter’s principal. The interest bill, unfortunately, does not wait for the transformer.
One more thing happened this week, and it happened in Tokyo. Jensen Huang flew in, stood next to Japan’s industry minister, and pitched physical AI — robots and factories as the answer to our labor shortage. So within a single month, my country served as both the BIS’s warning label and NVIDIA’s showroom. Both uses are legitimate. Holding those two facts at once is what this essay is about.
This is not a bubble call. I don’t have the evidence for one, and neither, frankly, do the people making them. The question I want to ask is narrower and more useful: how much time passes between the day the money is borrowed and the day electricity turns it into cash — and who is watching that gap.
Two Billion Dollars a Day
Add up the 2026 capital expenditure guidance from Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Meta and you get $695–725 billion. Call it up to two billion dollars a day. Amazon plans roughly $200 billion, Microsoft about $190 billion for the calendar year, Alphabet $180–190 billion, Meta $125–145 billion.
Not all of it is AI datacenters. Amazon’s number includes logistics, robotics, and satellites, and the four companies don’t disclose servers, chips, land, and power equipment under any common definition. Japan’s Marubeni Research Institute estimates roughly 70 percent goes to AI infrastructure, but that’s an analyst’s estimate, not an auditable figure.
The scale survives the caveats. The BIS calculates that US datacenter spending already accounts for about 0.5 percent of GDP, heading toward 0.8–1.3 percent if an additional $100–225 billion a year materializes over the next five years. Goldman Sachs Research noted that $700 billion in hyperscaler capex would match, in GDP terms, the peak of the late-1990s telecom buildout. The top end of the guidance exceeds it.
What that money buys is the interesting part. Not software. Buildings, transformers, substations, water, cooling. AI has walked out of the software industry and into heavy industry, where the binding constraints are permits and construction schedules, not code.
Which is why Huang’s Tokyo pitch matters beyond the photo op. His “physical AI” is about demand — machines doing work in the physical world. The grid queues and transformer backlogs in this essay are about supply — the physical world’s capacity to power the machines. Different topics, same direction. The main arena of AI has moved off the screen. Demand and constraint both live in the physical world now.
What the Forty Percent Actually Counts
The number that anchored that Japanese broadcast — and a lot of English coverage — is “40 percent of datacenters are delayed.” Quoted bare, it sounds like the industry is seizing up worldwide. The actual measurement is narrower.
The analysis, reported by the Financial Times and based on SynMax’s satellite imagery cross-checked against IIR Energy’s permit and announcement data, found that about 40 percent of US datacenter projects scheduled to open in 2026 are running at least three months behind. A private estimate, US-only, forward-looking. Not 40 percent of the world’s datacenters, and not existing facilities.
SynMax adds that more than 60 percent of projects slated for 2027 completion haven’t broken ground. The International Energy Agency, using a different lens, estimates that about 20 percent of globally planned datacenter projects face delay risk through 2030 unless grid constraints ease. Forty and twenty don’t contradict each other; they count different things over different periods.
The decimal points matter less than the structure they reveal. Demand for buildings is outrunning the supply of electricity and construction capacity. The IEA says a new transmission line in an advanced economy takes four to eight years. Lead times for transformers and cables have doubled in three years. You can buy the land and finish the server hall, and without an interconnection, revenue is zero.
Read that way, “40 percent delayed” is not evidence of collapsing demand. If anything it’s evidence of demand hitting the physical world’s speed limit.
That is not proof of a bubble. But the repayment schedule does not wait for the transformer’s delivery date.
Whose Money Builds the Datacenter
Big Tech generates enormous operating cash flow. When capex growth outruns it, the toolkit expands: bonds, leases, securitization, joint ventures, private credit. The expansion is already fact — BIS data shows hyperscaler bond issuance topped $100 billion in 2025, much of it beyond five-year maturities.
The forward numbers are larger. Morgan Stanley Research, cited in the FSB’s report, projects $2.9 trillion of global datacenter capex for 2025–2028 excluding power. Of that, $1.5 trillion — about 52 percent — would come from external capital, with broadly defined private credit supplying $800 billion, roughly 28 percent. A projection, not a tally, and it includes asset-based finance and infrastructure lending.
Private credit is not the villain of this story. Funds lending directly to borrowers can tailor terms around construction risk, power risk, tenant concentration — things a syndicated bank loan handles clumsily. Long-term contracted datacenters and asset-backed finance genuinely fit.
Look at the flagship deal. Meta and Blue Owl’s Hyperion datacenter joint venture: about $27 billion in total development cost, Blue Owl funds holding 80 percent, Meta 20 percent. Blue Owl contributes about $7 billion in cash; Meta contributed land and construction-in-progress and took a roughly $3 billion one-time distribution. Part of Blue Owl’s capital is being raised as debt through a private placement to PIMCO and other bond investors. Note what this is: a $27 billion development JV, not a $27 billion private credit loan.
It’s a template, not a one-off. In May, Google and Blackstone announced a joint venture to run a US TPU cloud business — Blackstone committing $5 billion of initial equity, targeting 500 megawatts by 2027. The BIS has a name for economically debt-like obligations that live outside corporate balance sheets in these structures: shadow borrowing. What the JV’s total debt load will be, the founding announcements don’t say.
Here is the problem with boxes. Ten JVs and SPVs look like diversification. But if the ultimate tenants are the same handful of companies, demand risk hasn’t been spread; it has been repackaged. Trace the payment streams behind a “diversified” fund portfolio and you may keep arriving at the same names: OpenAI, Microsoft, Meta, Amazon, Alphabet.
The stock is growing fast from a small base. BIS: AI-sector private credit outstanding passed $200 billion by 2025, nearly 8 percent of the total book. New originations exceeded $40 billion in 2025, up from about $3 billion in 2010 — yet still only about 4 percent of new direct lending. Not yet systemic. No longer negligible.
PIK, or the Art of Not Looking
When construction slips, contracts that tie fees to delivery slip with it. Expected cash doesn’t arrive; cash interest gets hard to pay. Enter payment-in-kind.
The mechanics are simple. Owe 10 in interest on 100 of principal, can’t pay cash, so the principal becomes 110. Next period, interest accrues on 110. Interest earning interest. You buy time; the meter runs.
PIK has honest uses. If a datacenter is six months late but the tenant contract and the demand are intact, capitalizing a few coupons beats forcing a workout. Lender and borrower can both be acting rationally.
But PIK changes what income looks like. The fund’s books show interest revenue rising while no cash comes in — and distributions to the fund’s own investors are paid in cash. If the delay was never really about construction — if it’s soft demand or a weakening tenant — PIK doesn’t solve the problem. It reschedules the moment anyone has to admit it.
This is where I hear the rhyme with oigashi. Japan’s banks in the 1990s were not, for the most part, committing fraud. They were exercising discretion, one reasonable extension at a time, inside a system where nobody’s incentives pointed toward recognition. A rhyme is not a repetition — I’ll come back to the differences — but the shape of the incentive is the same: every individual decision to wait is defensible, and the sum of them is a decade.
The FSB’s numbers put boundaries on the current version. About 12 percent of private credit loans are actually using PIK, roughly half of that through PIK toggles — clauses letting borrowers switch cash coupons to PIK after origination. That’s usage, not mere availability; the report tracks the two separately. A study of US BDC loans the FSB cites finds that exercising a toggle is associated with a 1–2 percentage point higher probability of delinquency the following quarter. Against an unconditional delinquency rate of 3 percent, that is not a small association — though association is all it is.
Defaults tell the same double story. Formal defaults run near 1 percent. Include selective defaults — restructurings on non-arm’s-length terms, done to rescue the borrower — and the trailing 12-month rate is about 5 percent. Broader proxies that count distressed exchanges run higher still.
And the marks. Private loans don’t trade daily; they’re valued quarterly, by managers who can amend terms with borrowers along the way. That’s resilience — no forced fire sales — and it is also, simultaneously, a mechanism for finding out later.
The Wrong Question About 2008
The similarities are real. Money flowed in the 2000s on the premise that house prices only rose; capex compounds today on the premise that AI demand only grows. SPVs, securitization, and private lending stack up in both stories until the final resting place of the risk is hard to see from outside.
The differences are bigger. In 2008
